text_summary: Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel) is a second-generation autologous anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy developed by Novartis and the University of Pennsylvania. It was the first CAR-T product to receive FDA approval in August 2017 for pediatric and young adult relapsed/refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (r/r B-ALL), later expanded to diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) and follicular lymphoma (FL). The construct utilizes the well-characterized FMC63 murine scFv targeting CD19 in a VL-linker-VH orientation with a (G4S)3 flexible linker. The signaling architecture employs a CD8-alpha hinge and transmembrane domain fused to a 4-1BB (CD137) costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain containing three immunoreceptor tyrosine-based activation motifs (ITAMs). The 4-1BB costimulatory domain promotes preferential central memory T-cell differentiation, sustained proliferation, and prolonged in vivo persistence compared to CD28-costimulated CARs. Manufacturing uses a lentiviral vector (self-inactivating HIV-1-derived) transduction system with a typical vector copy number of 1-3 copies per cell, providing stable transgene integration with low insertional mutagenesis risk. The ELIANA pivotal trial demonstrated an 82% overall remission rate in pediatric B-ALL with 60% of responders remaining in remission at 12 months. Key safety considerations include cytokine release syndrome (CRS) in 77% of patients (47% Grade 3-4) and neurotoxicity (ICANS) in approximately 40%. The product requires cryopreservation and has a complex 22-day manufacturing process with a defined vein-to-vein time of approximately 3-4 weeks.
name: Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel)
known_toxicities: CRS (77%, 47% Grade 3-4), neurotoxicity/ICANS (40%), B-cell aplasia, hypogammaglobulinemia, prolonged cytopenias, secondary T-cell malignancies (boxed warning added 2023)

text_summary: Yescarta (axicabtagene ciloleucel, axi-cel) is a second-generation autologous anti-CD19 CAR-T therapy developed by Kite Pharma (now Gilead). It received FDA approval in October 2017 for relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma and was subsequently expanded to follicular lymphoma. The construct uses the same FMC63 murine scFv as Kymriah but diverges critically in its costimulatory architecture, employing a CD28 costimulatory domain rather than 4-1BB. This design choice produces a characteristically rapid and potent initial T-cell expansion with robust cytokine production, though with somewhat reduced long-term persistence compared to 4-1BB constructs. The signaling cassette consists of a CD28 hinge, CD28 transmembrane domain, CD28 costimulatory endodomain, and CD3-zeta activation domain. Manufacturing utilizes a gamma-retroviral vector (MSGV1 backbone) with a rapid ex vivo activation and transduction protocol achieving a shorter 17-day vein-to-vein time compared to lentiviral approaches. The ZUMA-1 pivotal trial demonstrated a 72% objective response rate with 51% complete responses in DLBCL, and the ZUMA-7 trial later showed superiority over standard-of-care salvage chemotherapy and autologous transplant in second-line LBCL. CD28 costimulation drives higher peak CAR-T expansion and serum cytokine levels, correlating with both efficacy and a higher incidence of severe CRS and neurotoxicity. Approximately 93% of patients experience CRS (13% Grade 3+) and 64% develop neurotoxicity (28% Grade 3+). The faster kinetics of CD28-based CARs make Yescarta particularly relevant for patients with rapidly progressing disease who may not tolerate the slower onset kinetics of 4-1BB constructs.
name: Yescarta (axicabtagene ciloleucel)
known_toxicities: CRS (93%, 13% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity/ICANS (64%, 28% Grade 3+), B-cell aplasia, hypogammaglobulinemia, macrophage activation syndrome, secondary T-cell malignancies (boxed warning added 2023)

text_summary: Tecartus (brexucabtagene autoleucel, brexu-cel) is a second-generation autologous anti-CD19 CAR-T therapy developed by Kite Pharma (Gilead) and FDA-approved in July 2020 for relapsed/refractory mantle cell lymphoma (MCL), later expanded to adult B-ALL. While sharing the same CAR construct design as Yescarta (FMC63 scFv, CD28 costimulation, CD3-zeta signaling, gamma-retroviral vector), Tecartus differs critically in its manufacturing process. It incorporates a T-cell enrichment step that depletes circulating tumor cells (malignant B cells) from the leukapheresis product prior to activation and transduction. This is essential for MCL and B-ALL patients who frequently have circulating malignant cells that express CD19 and could be inadvertently transduced, creating tumor cells that mask their target antigen. The enrichment process uses anti-CD4 and anti-CD8 magnetic bead selection to isolate T cells with high purity. The ZUMA-2 pivotal trial in MCL demonstrated an 87% overall response rate with 62% complete responses, representing a transformative outcome for a historically difficult-to-treat lymphoma subtype. In the ZUMA-3 trial for adult B-ALL, the overall complete remission rate was 71%. The toxicity profile mirrors Yescarta with high rates of CRS (91%, 15% Grade 3+) and neurotoxicity (63%, 31% Grade 3+). The manufacturing enrichment step adds approximately 1-2 days to the process but is critical for product purity and preventing antigen masking in leukemic indications. Brexu-cel represents an important lesson in how manufacturing modifications, rather than construct redesign, can adapt a CAR-T platform to new disease contexts.
name: Tecartus (brexucabtagene autoleucel)
known_toxicities: CRS (91%, 15% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity/ICANS (63%, 31% Grade 3+), B-cell aplasia, febrile neutropenia, prolonged cytopenias, tumor lysis syndrome, secondary T-cell malignancies (boxed warning added 2023)

text_summary: Breyanzi (lisocabtagene maraleucel, liso-cel) is a second-generation autologous anti-CD19 CAR-T therapy developed by Juno Therapeutics (Bristol-Myers Squibb) and FDA-approved in February 2021 for relapsed/refractory large B-cell lymphoma, subsequently expanded to second-line LBCL, CLL, and SLL. Breyanzi is distinguished by its defined-composition design, administering CD4+ and CD8+ CAR-T cells at a fixed 1:1 ratio in separately manufactured components. This controlled composition approach aims to reduce variability in clinical outcomes and improve the therapeutic index by ensuring consistent CD4/CD8 ratios regardless of patient starting material heterogeneity. The construct uses the FMC63 murine anti-CD19 scFv coupled to a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain, with an IgG4 hinge and CD28 transmembrane domain representing a hybrid architecture. The IgG4 hinge includes mutations to prevent Fc receptor binding, reducing off-target activation. Manufacturing uses a lentiviral vector with separate transduction and expansion of CD4+ and CD8+ T-cell fractions followed by formulation at defined ratios. The TRANSCEND NHL 001 pivotal trial showed a 73% overall response rate with 53% complete responses in LBCL. Notably, Breyanzi demonstrates a more favorable safety profile than CD28-costimulated products, with CRS in 42% (2% Grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 30% (10% Grade 3+), making it the best-tolerated approved anti-CD19 CAR-T product. The defined composition approach enables outpatient administration in selected patients, a significant practical advance over competing products that typically require inpatient monitoring.
name: Breyanzi (lisocabtagene maraleucel)
known_toxicities: CRS (42%, 2% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity/ICANS (30%, 10% Grade 3+), B-cell aplasia, prolonged cytopenias, hypogammaglobulinemia, secondary T-cell malignancies (boxed warning added 2023)

text_summary: Abecma (idecabtagene vicleucel, ide-cel) is a second-generation autologous anti-BCMA CAR-T therapy developed by bluebird bio and Bristol-Myers Squibb, FDA-approved in March 2021 as the first BCMA-targeted CAR-T for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma after four or more prior lines of therapy. The construct targets B-cell maturation antigen (BCMA/TNFRSF17), a TNF receptor superfamily member selectively expressed on mature B cells and plasma cells, making it an ideal target for myeloma. The anti-BCMA scFv is derived from a murine antibody with high affinity for human BCMA. The signaling architecture uses a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta activation domain, optimized for the myeloma tumor microenvironment where sustained persistence is critical given the historically relapsing nature of the disease. The construct employs a CD8-alpha hinge and transmembrane domain with lentiviral vector delivery. The KarMMa pivotal trial demonstrated a 73% overall response rate with 33% complete responses at the recommended dose of 450 million CAR+ T cells, with a median progression-free survival of 8.8 months. Soluble BCMA (sBCMA) shed by ADAM10/17 metalloprotease cleavage can act as a decoy and compete for CAR binding, representing a unique resistance mechanism in BCMA-targeted therapies. CRS occurs in 84% of patients (5% Grade 3+) and neurotoxicity in 18% (3% Grade 3+). Abecma opened the door for CAR-T therapy in myeloma but has been somewhat overtaken by the more potent Carvykti in clinical practice, particularly following Carvykti's approval in earlier lines of therapy.
name: Abecma (idecabtagene vicleucel)
known_toxicities: CRS (84%, 5% Grade 3+), neurotoxicity/ICANS (18%, 3% Grade 3+), prolonged cytopenias, hypogammaglobulinemia, infections, secondary T-cell malignancies (boxed warning added 2023)

text_summary: Carvykti (ciltacabtagene autoleucel, cilta-cel) is a second-generation autologous anti-BCMA CAR-T therapy developed by Legend Biotech and Janssen (Johnson & Johnson), FDA-approved in February 2022 for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. Carvykti is distinguished by its innovative dual-epitope binding domain, which employs two llama-derived VHH nanobody domains (single-domain antibodies) connected in tandem that bind two distinct epitopes on BCMA simultaneously. This biparatopic design confers avidity-driven binding with an exceptionally high functional affinity, reducing the impact of soluble BCMA decoy interference and providing more robust target engagement than single-scFv approaches. The VHH nanobody domains are approximately 15 kDa each, significantly smaller than conventional scFvs (~25 kDa), enabling a more compact extracellular domain. The construct uses a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling endodomain with lentiviral vector delivery. The CARTITUDE-1 pivotal trial showed a remarkable 98% overall response rate with 83% stringent complete responses in heavily pretreated myeloma patients, with a median progression-free survival of 34.9 months, substantially exceeding all other available therapies. The CARTITUDE-4 trial subsequently demonstrated superiority over standard-of-care in 1-3 prior lines, leading to an expanded indication. CRS occurs in 95% (4% Grade 3+) and a unique delayed neurotoxicity syndrome including movement and neurocognitive treatment-emergent adverse events (MNTs) affects approximately 12% of patients, including parkinsonian features and cranial nerve palsies that can emerge weeks to months post-infusion. This delayed neurotoxicity pattern is distinct from acute ICANS and may be related to the high potency and persistence of cilta-cel.
name: Carvykti (ciltacabtagene autoleucel)
known_toxicities: CRS (95%, 4% Grade 3+), delayed neurotoxicity/MNTs (12%, parkinsonian features, cranial nerve palsies), ICANS (17%), prolonged cytopenias, hypogammaglobulinemia, secondary T-cell malignancies (boxed warning added 2023)

text_summary: UCART19 is a pioneering allogeneic (off-the-shelf) anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy developed by Cellectis using their proprietary TALEN gene-editing technology. Unlike autologous CAR-T products that require patient-specific manufacturing, UCART19 is manufactured from healthy donor T cells that undergo multiplex gene editing to eliminate graft-versus-host disease (GvHD) risk and enable persistence in immunocompetent recipients. The construct employs TALEN-mediated knockout of the TCR-alpha constant (TRAC) locus to ablate endogenous TCR expression, preventing allogeneic T cells from recognizing and attacking host tissues. Simultaneously, the CD52 gene is disrupted to confer resistance to alemtuzumab, a lymphodepleting anti-CD52 monoclonal antibody used in the conditioning regimen to prevent host immune rejection of the allogeneic product. The CAR construct itself uses an anti-CD19 scFv (4G7 clone) with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain, integrated into the disrupted TRAC locus for regulated expression. The allogeneic approach offers transformative manufacturing advantages: a single donor manufacturing run can produce hundreds of doses, dramatically reducing cost (projected <$50,000 vs. $373,000-475,000 for autologous products) and enabling immediate product availability. The CALM and PALL clinical trials in B-ALL demonstrated proof-of-concept with molecular remissions achieved, though persistence was limited to 28-42 days due to host immune rejection despite lymphodepletion. Key challenges include limited durability requiring repeat dosing or bridging to transplant, residual TCR+ cells causing GvHD risk, and the need for intensive lymphodepletion with associated infectious complications. UCART19 represents the first-in-human allogeneic CAR-T program and has established the foundational gene-editing and manufacturing framework for the broader allogeneic CAR-T field.
name: UCART19
vector_type: lentiviral + TALEN gene editing (TRAC KO, CD52 KO)
known_toxicities: CRS, GvHD risk (residual TCR+ cells), prolonged cytopenias from alemtuzumab conditioning, infections from lymphodepletion, limited persistence (28-42 days)

text_summary: CTX110 is a CRISPR-Cas9-edited allogeneic anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy developed by Caribou Biosciences using their proprietary chRDNA (CRISPR hybrid RNA-DNA) guide technology. The construct is manufactured from healthy donor T cells with CRISPR-mediated knockout of the TRAC locus (to prevent GvHD) and beta-2-microglobulin (B2M) gene (to eliminate MHC class I surface expression and reduce host NK- and T-cell-mediated rejection). The dual gene knockout approach represents an advancement over TCR-only knockout strategies by addressing both GvHD and host-versus-graft rejection. However, B2M knockout and loss of MHC class I can paradoxically increase susceptibility to NK cell-mediated lysis through missing-self recognition, necessitating careful lymphodepletion strategies. The CAR cassette is inserted at the TRAC locus using adeno-associated virus (AAV)-mediated homology-directed repair, enabling expression from the endogenous TCR promoter for physiological regulation of CAR expression, which may reduce tonic signaling and T-cell exhaustion. The CRISPR editing uses Caribou's chRDNA guides, which incorporate DNA nucleotides at specific positions in the guide RNA to improve specificity and reduce off-target editing. The CARBON phase 1 trial in r/r B-cell NHL demonstrated clinical activity with complete responses achieved, though durability remained limited compared to autologous products. CTX110 uses a CD19-targeted scFv with 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling. The program represents one of the most advanced CRISPR-based allogeneic CAR-T approaches and has provided critical safety data on the clinical use of CRISPR gene editing in cellular therapy.
name: CTX110
vector_type: CRISPR-Cas9 (chRDNA guides) + AAV HDR knock-in (TRAC KO, B2M KO)
known_toxicities: CRS, GvHD (low incidence with TRAC KO), infections from lymphodepletion, limited persistence due to host immune rejection, potential NK cell-mediated lysis from B2M KO

text_summary: CB-010 is a next-generation CRISPR-edited allogeneic anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy developed by Caribou Biosciences that incorporates PD-1 checkpoint knockout to enhance antitumor activity within the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment. Building on the CTX110 platform, CB-010 features triple CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing: TRAC knockout (GvHD prevention), B2M knockout (immune evasion from host T cells), and PDCD1 (PD-1) knockout to prevent PD-L1-mediated T-cell exhaustion in the tumor microenvironment. The PD-1 knockout represents a critical innovation addressing a major mechanism of CAR-T cell dysfunction, particularly relevant in lymphoma where PD-L1 expression on tumor cells and the microenvironment can suppress CAR-T effector function. The CAR cassette is knocked into the TRAC locus via AAV-mediated homology-directed repair, maintaining the regulated expression advantage of site-specific integration. Preclinical data demonstrated that PD-1 knockout CAR-T cells showed enhanced cytotoxicity against PD-L1-positive tumor targets, increased cytokine production, and superior tumor control in xenograft models compared to PD-1-intact controls. The ANTLER phase 1 trial in r/r B-cell NHL showed encouraging early clinical data with a 78% complete response rate in evaluable patients at the first dose level, potentially superior to CTX110 results and suggesting that checkpoint knockout meaningfully enhances allogeneic CAR-T efficacy. However, PD-1 knockout raises theoretical safety concerns about autoimmunity and uncontrolled T-cell proliferation, though clinical data to date have not shown concerning autoimmune signals. CB-010 represents the convergence of allogeneic manufacturing, gene editing, and checkpoint engineering in a single product, providing a template for the next wave of engineered cellular therapies.
name: CB-010
vector_type: CRISPR-Cas9 (chRDNA guides) + AAV HDR knock-in (TRAC KO, B2M KO, PDCD1 KO)
known_toxicities: CRS, infections from lymphodepletion, limited persistence, theoretical autoimmune risk from PD-1 KO, potential NK cell lysis from B2M KO

text_summary: FT819 is the first clinical-stage iPSC-derived anti-CD19 CAR-T cell therapy, developed by Fate Therapeutics. It represents a fundamentally different approach to allogeneic CAR-T manufacturing by deriving T cells from a clonal master induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) line rather than primary donor T cells. The iPSC line is engineered at the pluripotent stage with the anti-CD19 CAR construct knocked into the TRAC locus, achieving uniform, single-copy CAR integration in every cell. The TRAC disruption simultaneously eliminates TCR expression (preventing GvHD) and places CAR expression under endogenous TCR promoter control. The iPSC-to-T-cell differentiation protocol generates a unique T-cell product with an innate-like phenotype resembling invariant natural killer T (iNKT) cells, with distinct effector mechanisms compared to conventional alpha-beta T cells. This approach offers transformative manufacturing scalability: a single iPSC clone can theoretically generate an unlimited number of doses with identical genetic characteristics, enabling true off-the-shelf availability with perfect lot-to-lot consistency. The iPSC platform also allows iterative engineering at the clonal level, including additional gene edits for enhanced persistence, immune evasion, and functionality that would be impractical in primary T-cell manufacturing. Early clinical data from the phase 1 trial showed safety and proof-of-concept anti-leukemic activity, though potency and persistence required optimization. Key challenges include the complex and lengthy differentiation process (approximately 40-60 days from iPSC to mature T cells), ensuring complete pluripotency elimination to prevent teratoma risk, and achieving sufficient T-cell maturity and effector function comparable to primary T-cell-derived products. FT819 establishes the feasibility of iPSC-derived CAR-T therapy and opens the path for next-generation iPSC products with multiple engineered enhancements.
name: FT819
vector_type: CRISPR knock-in at TRAC locus in iPSC (clonal master cell bank)
known_toxicities: CRS (low incidence in early data), theoretical teratoma risk from residual iPSC, limited persistence, infections from lymphodepletion conditioning

text_summary: The anti-CD22 CAR-T cell therapy using the m971 scFv represents a critical alternative target for B-cell malignancies, particularly for patients who relapse after CD19-directed therapies due to CD19 antigen loss, which occurs in approximately 30-60% of post-CD19-CAR relapses. CD22 is a B-cell lineage-restricted sialic acid-binding immunoglobulin-like lectin (Siglec-2) expressed on most B-cell malignancies. The m971 scFv was derived from a fully human antibody isolated from a phage display library and binds the membrane-proximal Ig-like domains of CD22 (domains 5-7), which are less susceptible to alternative splicing and more consistently expressed than the membrane-distal domains. The construct employs a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain with a CD8-alpha hinge and transmembrane domain, delivered via lentiviral vector. A critical challenge for CD22-targeted CARs is site density sensitivity: CD22 is expressed at lower surface density than CD19 (approximately 5,000-10,000 vs. 10,000-30,000 molecules per cell), and CD22 CAR efficacy is highly dependent on target antigen density. Dim CD22 expression or partial downregulation can lead to subthreshold CAR activation and treatment failure. The NCI pediatric B-ALL trial demonstrated a 73% complete remission rate as a single agent, with particular value in CD19-negative relapse settings. However, durability has been limited with a median remission duration of approximately 6 months, attributed in part to CD22 density modulation. This has motivated development of CD19/CD22 bispecific approaches to prevent single-antigen escape. The m971 scFv is fully human in origin, conferring lower immunogenicity risk compared to murine-derived CD19 binders, which may enable retreatment strategies.
name: Anti-CD22 CAR-T (m971 scFv)
known_toxicities: CRS (moderate), limited durability (~6 months median remission), CD22 density-dependent efficacy, B-cell aplasia, hypogammaglobulinemia

text_summary: The CD19/CD22 bispecific tandem CAR-T construct represents a dual-targeting strategy designed to overcome the major clinical challenge of single-antigen escape following monospecific CAR-T therapy. Approximately 30-60% of patients relapsing after CD19-targeted CAR-T exhibit CD19 loss or downregulation, and sequential CD22-targeted therapy faces similar resistance through CD22 density modulation. The bispecific tandem CAR encodes two scFv binding domains (anti-CD19 FMC63 and anti-CD22 m971) arranged in a single continuous polypeptide chain connected by a flexible glycine-serine linker. This tandem design differs from bicistronic approaches (which express two separate CARs from a single vector) by ensuring equimolar co-expression and co-localization of both binding domains in every CAR molecule. The tandem scFv architecture enables simultaneous engagement of CD19 and CD22 on the same target cell, increasing avidity and enabling activation even when individual antigen densities are subthreshold for monospecific CAR activation. The construct utilizes 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling with lentiviral delivery. Clinical trials at multiple centers (NCI, Stanford, UPenn) have demonstrated feasibility with overall response rates of 70-88% in r/r B-ALL, with responses observed in patients with CD19-dim or CD19-negative disease. Critically, the rate of antigen-negative relapse appears lower than with monospecific approaches, with dual antigen loss being rare. However, the larger transgene size (~2.4 kb for tandem scFvs vs. ~1.5 kb for single scFv) can reduce viral titers and transduction efficiency. Optimization of linker length, scFv orientation (CD19-CD22 vs. CD22-CD19), and relative binding domain geometry remains an active area of engineering. This approach has established dual-targeting as a key paradigm for overcoming antigen escape in CAR-T therapy.
name: CD19/CD22 bispecific tandem CAR-T
known_toxicities: CRS (moderate), neurotoxicity, B-cell aplasia, hypogammaglobulinemia, reduced transduction efficiency from large transgene

text_summary: MCARH109 is an investigational anti-FcRH5 (Fc receptor-homolog 5, also known as FCRL5 or CD307) CAR-T cell therapy developed at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center (MSKCC) for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma. FcRH5 is a member of the Fc receptor-like family that is broadly and uniformly expressed on malignant plasma cells in myeloma with minimal expression on non-B-cell tissues, offering potential advantages over BCMA as a target. Unlike BCMA, FcRH5 is not known to be significantly shed from the cell surface, potentially eliminating the soluble antigen decoy problem that complicates BCMA-targeted therapies. FcRH5 expression is also maintained in BCMA-low or BCMA-loss myeloma, making it a valuable alternative or sequential target for patients who fail BCMA-directed therapies. The MCARH109 construct uses a humanized anti-FcRH5 scFv derived from antibody optimization campaigns, combined with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta activation domain delivered by lentiviral vector. The phase 1 clinical trial at MSKCC demonstrated proof-of-concept antimyeloma activity with objective responses including stringent complete responses in heavily pretreated patients, many of whom had received prior BCMA-targeted therapy. The safety profile showed manageable CRS and low rates of neurotoxicity. FcRH5 expression on normal B cells means that B-cell aplasia is an expected on-target off-tumor effect, similar to CD19 CAR-T in lymphoma. MCARH109 is part of a broader wave of non-BCMA myeloma targets (including GPRC5D, FcRH5, and SLAMF7) being developed to address BCMA-refractory disease and enable sequential immunotherapy strategies in myeloma.
name: MCARH109 (anti-FcRH5 CAR-T)
known_toxicities: CRS (manageable), low neurotoxicity, B-cell aplasia (on-target off-tumor), hypogammaglobulinemia, cytopenias

text_summary: The anti-GPRC5D CAR-T cell therapy targets G protein-coupled receptor class C group 5 member D, an orphan GPCR selectively expressed on malignant plasma cells in multiple myeloma with limited normal tissue expression (primarily hair follicle cells). GPRC5D has emerged as a high-priority alternative target to BCMA in myeloma, particularly for patients who progress after BCMA-directed therapies. The construct utilizes a VHH nanobody (single-domain antibody) derived from llama immunization as the antigen-binding domain rather than a conventional scFv. The VHH format offers several advantages: smaller size (~15 kDa vs. ~25 kDa scFv) enabling improved cell surface expression and reduced steric hindrance, absence of light chain mispairing artifacts, superior stability and refolding capacity, and the ability to access epitopes in recessed binding pockets of GPCRs that may be inaccessible to larger scFv domains. The compact VHH domain is particularly relevant for GPRC5D, whose extracellular domain is relatively small and may not accommodate bulky scFv binding. The construct employs 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling with lentiviral delivery. GPRC5D expression is maintained in most BCMA-low or BCMA-loss cases, and surface expression is not affected by gamma-secretase-mediated shedding, eliminating the soluble antigen decoy problem. Multiple clinical programs targeting GPRC5D are advancing, including both CAR-T and bispecific antibody approaches (talquetamab). Early clinical data show deep responses including MRD-negative complete remissions in heavily pretreated patients, including those with prior BCMA-targeted therapy failure. A unique on-target off-tumor toxicity is nail and skin changes including dysgeusia (taste alteration), skin desquamation, and nail dystrophy, reflecting GPRC5D expression on keratinocytes.
name: Anti-GPRC5D CAR-T (VHH nanobody)
known_toxicities: CRS, dysgeusia (taste alteration), skin desquamation, nail dystrophy, cytopenias, infections

text_summary: P-BCMA-101 is an investigational anti-BCMA CAR-T cell therapy developed by Poseida Therapeutics using a non-viral PiggyBac DNA transposon delivery system and a novel Centyrin-based binding domain instead of a conventional scFv. Centyrins are small (~10 kDa) engineered protein scaffolds derived from the human fibronectin type III (FN3) domain of tenascin-C, providing a fully human, non-immunoglobulin binding domain. The anti-BCMA Centyrin was selected from combinatorial libraries for high-affinity BCMA binding and offers advantages over scFvs including smaller size, greater thermostability, absence of disulfide bonds enabling more robust folding, and fully human origin eliminating anti-mouse antibody responses. The PiggyBac transposon system represents a paradigm shift from viral vector delivery: the CAR transgene flanked by inverted terminal repeats is co-delivered with a hyperactive PiggyBac transposase via electroporation, enabling stable genomic integration without the need for viral vector manufacturing. This dramatically reduces manufacturing cost and complexity while enabling larger transgene payloads. PiggyBac integrates preferentially at TTAA sites with a relatively random genomic distribution, though with some bias toward transcriptionally active regions. The construct uses a 4-1BB costimulatory domain and CD3-zeta signaling domain with a truncated EGFR (EGFRt) safety switch enabling cetuximab-mediated depletion if needed. Early clinical data from the phase 1 trial showed antimyeloma activity with responses in heavily pretreated patients, though the response depth was more modest than observed with viral vector-delivered BCMA CARs. The T-cell product is enriched for stem cell memory (Tscm) phenotype through manufacturing optimization, aiming to improve long-term persistence. P-BCMA-101 demonstrates the viability of non-viral, non-scFv approaches to CAR-T therapy and their potential to reduce cost-of-goods.
name: P-BCMA-101
scfv_origin: Anti-BCMA Centyrin (fully human FN3-based scaffold, non-scFv)
known_toxicities: CRS (low incidence), cytopenias, infections, hypogammaglobulinemia, theoretical insertional mutagenesis from transposon integration

text_summary: bb21217 is an investigational anti-BCMA CAR-T cell therapy developed by bluebird bio (now 2seventy bio) and Bristol-Myers Squibb as a next-generation improvement over Abecma (ide-cel). The construct shares the same CAR design as Abecma (anti-BCMA scFv, 4-1BB costimulation, CD3-zeta signaling, lentiviral delivery) but incorporates a critical manufacturing modification: ex vivo treatment of CAR-T cells with the PI3K inhibitor bb007 (a selective PI3Kdelta inhibitor) during the expansion phase. PI3K signaling during T-cell activation drives terminal effector differentiation, reducing the stem cell memory and central memory T-cell populations that are critical for long-term in vivo persistence. By inhibiting PI3K during manufacturing, bb21217 enriches for T cells with a less-differentiated memory phenotype characterized by high expression of CD62L, CCR7, TCF-1, and LEF-1, transcription factors associated with self-renewal capacity and sustained antitumor immunity. Preclinical studies demonstrated that PI3K inhibitor-treated CAR-T cells showed enhanced engraftment, persistence, and tumor control in serial rechallenge xenograft models compared to standard manufactured product. The CRB-402 phase 1 clinical trial in relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma showed clinical responses with evidence of prolonged CAR-T persistence compared to historical ide-cel data, supporting the hypothesis that manufacturing conditions critically influence in vivo performance. bb21217 represents an important proof-of-concept that pharmacological modulation during ex vivo expansion can improve CAR-T cell fitness without requiring construct redesign. This approach has influenced the broader field, with multiple programs now incorporating small molecule modulators (PI3K inhibitors, AKT inhibitors, GSK3-beta inhibitors) during manufacturing to enhance the quality of the final cell product.
name: bb21217
vector_type: lentiviral (SIN HIV-1 derived) + PI3Ki (bb007) during expansion
known_toxicities: CRS (moderate), neurotoxicity (low), cytopenias, infections, hypogammaglobulinemia

text_summary: Anti-CD33 CAR-T cell therapy targets the sialic acid-binding receptor CD33 (Siglec-3), a myeloid differentiation antigen expressed on approximately 85-90% of acute myeloid leukemia (AML) blasts. CD33 has been clinically validated as an AML target through gemtuzumab ozogamicin (Mylotarg), an anti-CD33 antibody-drug conjugate. However, CD33-directed CAR-T therapy faces a fundamental biological challenge not encountered in B-cell malignancies: fratricidal killing. CD33 is expressed not only on AML blasts but also on normal myeloid progenitors, monocytes, and critically on a subset of activated T cells themselves. During ex vivo manufacturing, CAR-T cells can recognize and kill CD33-expressing T cells within the product, leading to poor expansion, reduced viability, and inadequate dose generation. This fratricide problem has been a major obstacle to clinical translation. Proposed solutions include: CRISPR-mediated knockout of CD33 on the CAR-T cells themselves (making them invisible to their own CAR), use of rapidly switchable or logic-gated CAR designs that are inactive during manufacturing, anti-CD33 antibody blocking during expansion, and selection for CD33-negative T-cell subsets. The construct typically uses an anti-CD33 scFv (such as the My96 or gemtuzumab-derived clone) with CD28 or 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling. Beyond fratricide, prolonged on-target off-tumor myelosuppression is a major clinical concern, as CD33 is expressed on normal hematopoietic stem/progenitor cells, potentially causing severe and sustained pancytopenia. Some approaches pair CD33 CAR-T with CD33-knockout hematopoietic stem cell transplant, where the edited stem cells are resistant to CAR-T killing, enabling selective elimination of CD33-positive leukemia while preserving engineered hematopoiesis. This combination strategy illustrates the emerging paradigm of paired CAR-T and gene-edited transplant for myeloid malignancies.
name: Anti-CD33 CAR-T
vector_type: lentiviral (some programs add CRISPR CD33 KO on T cells)
known_toxicities: Fratricide during manufacturing, severe myelosuppression/pancytopenia (on-target off-tumor), CRS, infections, prolonged neutropenia requiring G-CSF or stem cell rescue

text_summary: Anti-CD70 CAR-T cell therapy targets CD70 (TNFSF7), a TNF superfamily member that is aberrantly expressed on multiple cancer types including acute myeloid leukemia, renal cell carcinoma (RCC), and certain lymphomas. CD70 is normally transiently expressed on activated T cells, B cells, and dendritic cells, where it functions as the ligand for CD27 to provide costimulatory signals. In cancer, constitutive CD70 overexpression promotes tumor immune evasion and survival signaling. Like CD33-targeted CARs in AML, anti-CD70 CAR-T faces the fratricide challenge because T cells transiently upregulate CD70 upon activation during manufacturing. To overcome this, the construct incorporates CRISPR-Cas9-mediated knockout of the CD70 gene in the CAR-T cells themselves, preventing self-recognition and fratricidal killing. This gene-editing solution simultaneously eliminates two problems: it prevents fratricide during manufacturing and may enhance in vivo persistence by preventing CD70-CD27-mediated activation-induced cell death. The construct uses an anti-CD70 scFv with 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling, delivered by lentiviral vector followed by CRISPR editing. Additional programs combine CD70 knockout with TRAC knockout for allogeneic applications. Clinical development spans both hematologic (AML) and solid tumor (RCC) indications, representing one of the few CAR-T targets with dual hematologic-solid tumor relevance. Early clinical data in RCC have shown promising antitumor activity, notable because CAR-T therapy has historically been challenging in solid tumors. The RCC application benefits from the relatively high and homogeneous CD70 expression in clear cell RCC and the kidney's accessibility to circulating T cells. Anti-CD70 CAR-T programs, particularly those with CRISPR-edited T cells, are advancing through multiple clinical trials and represent a convergence of gene editing and CAR-T engineering for both hematologic and solid malignancies.
name: Anti-CD70 CAR-T (CRISPR CD70 KO)
vector_type: lentiviral + CRISPR-Cas9 (CD70 KO on T cells, +/- TRAC KO for allogeneic)
known_toxicities: CRS, potential on-target off-tumor effects on activated immune cells, cytopenias, infections, theoretical autoimmune risk from disrupting CD70-CD27 axis

text_summary: Anti-GPC3 CAR-T cell therapy targets glypican-3 (GPC3), a heparan sulfate proteoglycan anchored to the cell membrane via glycosylphosphatidylinositol (GPI) linkage. GPC3 is an oncofetal antigen highly expressed in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC), where it is detectable in approximately 70-80% of tumors and correlates with poor prognosis. Critically, GPC3 expression is absent or minimal in normal adult liver tissue, providing a favorable therapeutic window for CAR-T targeting. HCC represents one of the most compelling solid tumor indications for CAR-T therapy due to the relatively homogeneous GPC3 expression and the liver's unique immunological environment with direct blood supply facilitating T-cell trafficking. The construct typically uses a humanized anti-GPC3 scFv derived from the GC33 or YP7 antibody clones, combined with CD28 or 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling. Multiple clinical trials in China and the United States have evaluated anti-GPC3 CAR-T in advanced HCC, with evidence of antitumor activity including partial responses and tumor necrosis on biopsy. However, responses have been limited by the immunosuppressive solid tumor microenvironment, including PD-L1-mediated exhaustion, TGF-beta signaling, regulatory T-cell infiltration, and physical barriers to T-cell penetration. Strategies to enhance anti-GPC3 CAR-T efficacy include armored constructs co-expressing dominant-negative TGF-beta receptor, PD-1 knockout, IL-15 or IL-21 cytokine co-expression, and combination with checkpoint inhibitors or locoregional therapies such as transarterial chemoembolization (TACE). Hepatic artery infusion of CAR-T cells has been explored to improve intrahepatic delivery. CRS can be complicated by hepatic dysfunction in patients with underlying cirrhosis. Anti-GPC3 CAR-T represents the leading CAR-T approach in HCC and a key testbed for solid tumor CAR-T strategies.
name: Anti-GPC3 CAR-T
scfv_origin: Humanized anti-GPC3 scFv (GC33 or YP7 clone)
known_toxicities: CRS (risk of hepatic flare in cirrhotic patients), elevated liver enzymes, on-target off-tumor risk in GPC3-expressing fetal tissues, limited efficacy from immunosuppressive TME

text_summary: Anti-mesothelin CAR-T cell therapy targets mesothelin (MSLN), a GPI-anchored cell surface glycoprotein overexpressed in mesothelioma (nearly 100%), pancreatic adenocarcinoma (~85%), ovarian cancer (~70%), and lung adenocarcinoma (~50%). Mesothelin plays a role in cell adhesion and is involved in tumor invasion through its interaction with MUC16 (CA-125). The SS1 scFv, derived from a murine anti-mesothelin antibody, is the most extensively studied binder in clinical CAR-T programs. The SS1-based CAR uses a high-affinity scFv (Kd ~5 nM) with either CD28 or 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling. Mesothelin is one of the most heavily investigated solid tumor CAR-T targets, with multiple clinical trials conducted at UPenn, NCI, MSKCC, and Chinese institutions. Early clinical experience revealed a critical barrier: the formation of human anti-mouse antibodies (HAMA) against the murine SS1 scFv, which limited retreatment potential and caused anaphylaxis in some patients. This led to the development of humanized and fully human anti-mesothelin binders. Clinical responses in mesothelioma have included evidence of tumor regression and prolonged stable disease, but complete responses have been rare, reflecting the challenges of solid tumor CAR-T therapy. The mesothelin program has served as a testing ground for multiple solid tumor engineering strategies including constitutive IL-12 secretion (armored CAR), PD-1 dominant-negative receptor co-expression, regional delivery (intrapleural infusion for mesothelioma), and mRNA-electroporated transiently-expressing CAR-T cells to manage safety risk. Intrapleural administration has shown particular promise, demonstrating improved tumor infiltration and reduced systemic toxicity compared to intravenous delivery. Low-level mesothelin expression on normal peritoneal, pleural, and pericardial mesothelial cells creates a potential on-target off-tumor risk, though clinically significant serositis has been manageable.
name: Anti-mesothelin CAR-T (SS1 scFv)
hinge_tm: CD8-alpha hinge + CD8-alpha TM or IgG4 hinge + CD28 TM
vector_type: lentiviral (also mRNA electroporation for transient expression)
known_toxicities: CRS, pleuritis/serositis (on-target off-tumor on mesothelial cells), HAMA with murine SS1, anaphylaxis risk on retreatment, limited solid tumor efficacy

text_summary: Anti-HER2 CAR-T cell therapy targets human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2/ERBB2), a receptor tyrosine kinase overexpressed in breast cancer (~20%), gastric cancer (~15-20%), and other carcinomas. HER2 CAR-T carries a cautionary history: in 2010, a patient died from fulminant CRS and pulmonary toxicity after infusion of a third-generation HER2 CAR-T (trastuzumab scFv, CD28+4-1BB) at high dose (10^10 cells). Toxicity was attributed to CAR-T recognition of low-level HER2 on pulmonary epithelium. This event profoundly shaped the field, underscoring the importance of affinity tuning and dose escalation. Subsequent programs employ the FRP5 scFv, which has lower affinity than trastuzumab-derived binders, enabling preferential recognition of HER2-overexpressing tumors while sparing normal tissues with physiological HER2 levels. This affinity-tuned approach exploits density-dependent CAR activation thresholds: lower-affinity binders require higher target density to trigger signaling, creating a therapeutic window. Current programs use second-generation constructs at lower doses (10^6-10^8 cells) and have demonstrated safety and modest activity in sarcomas, glioblastoma, and other HER2-positive solid tumors. The HER2 CAR-T experience established key safety principles for solid tumor CAR-T: affinity tuning, dose escalation, cautious lymphodepletion, and recognition that normal tissue antigen expression breadth is a critical risk factor.
name: Anti-HER2 CAR-T (FRP5 scFv, affinity-tuned)
scfv_origin: FRP5 (murine anti-HER2, lower affinity) or affinity-tuned trastuzumab variants
known_toxicities: CRITICAL HISTORY: fatal pulmonary toxicity from on-target off-tumor (trastuzumab scFv, 2010). Current programs with FRP5/affinity-tuned binders show improved safety. CRS, potential cardiotoxicity (HER2 on cardiomyocytes), limited solid tumor efficacy

text_summary: Anti-GD2 CAR-T cell therapy targets disialoganglioside GD2, a glycosphingolipid abundantly expressed on neuroblastoma (~100%), melanoma, osteosarcoma, and certain brain tumors. GD2 is thoroughly validated in pediatric oncology, targeted by dinutuximab (anti-GD2 mAb) for high-risk neuroblastoma. The 14g2a scFv recognizes the carbohydrate epitope of GD2 ganglioside. GD2 is unusual as a glycolipid target whose synthesis depends on the enzymatic pathway (GD2 synthase, ST8SIA1) rather than direct gene expression, making antigen loss through mutation extremely unlikely and providing inherent resistance to escape mechanisms that plague protein-targeted CARs. The construct uses 14g2a scFv with CD28 or 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling, with programs led by Baylor College of Medicine. A pioneering third-generation construct with CD28 and OX40 costimulation was among the first to demonstrate in vivo CAR-T persistence in pediatric neuroblastoma (Pule et al., Nature Medicine 2008). Current programs include armored variants co-expressing IL-15 and constructs with the C7R constitutively active IL-7 receptor. On-target off-tumor toxicity manifests as neuropathic pain due to GD2 expression on peripheral nerves. Clinical results in neuroblastoma have shown complete responses, and anti-GD2 CAR-T is being evaluated in DIPG with intracerebroventricular delivery showing encouraging early results.
name: Anti-GD2 CAR-T (14g2a scFv)
known_toxicities: Neuropathic pain (on-target off-tumor on peripheral nerves), CRS, tumor-associated inflammation in CNS tumors, GvHD risk with allogeneic variants, prolonged B-cell aplasia not expected

text_summary: Anti-Claudin18.2 CAR-T cell therapy targets Claudin 18 isoform 2 (CLDN18.2), a tight junction protein that is normally restricted to gastric mucosal cells but becomes aberrantly expressed and accessible on the surface of gastric adenocarcinoma (60-80%), pancreatic cancer (50-60%), and other gastrointestinal malignancies. In normal gastric epithelium, CLDN18.2 is sequestered within tight junctions and largely inaccessible to antibody or CAR-T recognition. However, during malignant transformation, loss of cellular polarity and tight junction disruption exposes CLDN18.2 on the accessible apical and basolateral cell surfaces, creating a tumor-selective therapeutic window. This unique biology makes CLDN18.2 one of the most promising solid tumor targets for CAR-T therapy, as the antigen is functionally tumor-specific despite being genetically present in normal tissue. The construct uses a humanized anti-CLDN18.2 scFv derived from the zolbetuximab (IMAB362) antibody lineage or independently derived binders, with 4-1BB costimulation and CD3-zeta signaling delivered by lentiviral vector. Clinical trials, predominantly conducted in China, have reported remarkable results for a solid tumor CAR-T program, including objective response rates of 57-83% in gastric and pancreatic cancers with some complete responses, substantially exceeding expectations for solid tumor CAR-T therapy. The CT041 program (Nanjing Legend/GenScript) has reported the most extensive clinical data, with durable responses observed in metastatic gastric cancer patients. CRS has been manageable, and the feared on-target gastric toxicity (mucosal inflammation, perforation risk) has been less severe than anticipated, likely because tight junction sequestration partially protects normal gastric CLDN18.2. The success of anti-CLDN18.2 CAR-T has been a landmark for the solid tumor CAR-T field, demonstrating that appropriate target selection can overcome many of the traditional barriers to solid tumor efficacy.
name: Anti-Claudin18.2 CAR-T
scfv_origin: Humanized anti-CLDN18.2 scFv (zolbetuximab-lineage or independently derived)
known_toxicities: CRS (manageable), potential gastric mucosal inflammation, nausea/vomiting, cytopenias from lymphodepletion, theoretical gastric perforation risk (not observed clinically)

text_summary: The IL-15 armored CAR-T construct represents a fourth-generation (armored/TRUCK) design incorporating autocrine interleukin-15 secretion to enhance T-cell persistence, particularly in immunosuppressive solid tumor microenvironments. The bicistronic vector co-expresses the CAR and a membrane-tethered or secreted IL-15 cassette, often as an IL-15/IL-15Ralpha sushi domain fusion (superagonist) mimicking physiological trans-presentation. IL-15 signals through IL-2/15Rbetagammac to activate JAK1/3-STAT5 and PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathways. Unlike IL-2, which expands regulatory T cells through CD25, IL-15 selectively supports effector and memory populations without Treg expansion, a critical advantage in the tumor microenvironment. The membrane-tethered design restricts cytokine action to autocrine/juxtacrine signaling, reducing systemic exposure while maintaining local T-cell support. Preclinical data show IL-15-armored CAR-T cells have superior expansion, persistence, and antitumor efficacy in serial rechallenge models. The IL-15 signal maintains a less-differentiated Tscm/Tcm phenotype and resists activation-induced cell death. Clinical translation across multiple targets (GD2, GPC3, mesothelin, CD19) shows enhanced persistence. Key safety considerations include theoretical risk of autonomous proliferation and lymphoproliferative complications from constitutive cytokine signaling, necessitating safety switch co-incorporation in some constructs.
name: IL-15 armored CAR-T (4th generation TRUCK)
target_antigen: Various (platform technology, applied to CD19, GD2, GPC3, mesothelin, etc.)
vector_type: lentiviral (bicistronic vector co-expressing CAR + IL-15 cassette)
known_toxicities: CRS, theoretical risk of autonomous T-cell proliferation, potential lymphoproliferative disorder, systemic cytokine toxicity if IL-15 release is excessive, target-dependent on-target off-tumor effects

text_summary: The inducible caspase-9 (iCasp9) safety switch CAR-T construct incorporates a suicide gene system for pharmacological ablation of CAR-T cells during unacceptable toxicity. The system consists of modified human caspase-9 fused to FKBP12 variant F36V that dimerizes upon exposure to the small molecule AP1903 (rimiducid). Rimiducid induces rapid caspase-9 homodimerization, triggering the intrinsic apoptotic cascade and eliminating >90% of iCasp9-expressing T cells within 30 minutes. Originally developed by Brenner and colleagues at Baylor for controlling GvHD in haploidentical transplant (Di Stasi et al., NEJM 2011), iCasp9 has been incorporated into multiple CAR-T constructs as a safety mechanism for solid tumor targets with on-target off-tumor risk, allogeneic products where GvHD remains a concern, and armored/checkpoint-knockout constructs where runaway proliferation is theoretically possible. The bicistronic vector co-expresses iCasp9 and the CAR separated by a T2A self-cleaving peptide. A key advantage over antibody-based depletion switches (truncated EGFR/cetuximab, CD20/rituximab) is speed: rimiducid-induced apoptosis occurs within minutes versus hours-to-days for ADCC. iCasp9 is the most clinically validated genetic safety switch in cellular therapy. Limitations include incomplete elimination (90-99% depletion), potential iCasp9-silenced escape variants, and irreversible loss of the therapeutic product upon activation.
name: iCasp9 safety switch CAR-T
target_antigen: Various (platform technology, applied across multiple targets)
known_toxicities: Base construct toxicities plus: rimiducid infusion reactions (mild), incomplete ablation (90-99% depletion), potential for iCasp9-silenced escape clones

name: Bispecific CD19/CD22 Tandem CAR
text_summary: Tandem bispecific CAR targeting both CD19 and CD22 using two scFvs (FMC63 for CD19, m971 for CD22) linked in a single CAR construct with 4-1BB costimulation. Designed to prevent CD19 antigen loss relapse — the primary mechanism of failure after CD19-only CAR-T. The tandem design allows simultaneous engagement of both antigens, with either antigen sufficient to trigger killing. In clinical testing: ORR 78% in patients who relapsed after prior CD19 CAR-T with documented CD19 loss. Only 8% of patients showed dual antigen escape (vs 30-60% single antigen escape with mono-specific). Key engineering choice: FMC63 (high affinity, 0.3 nM Kd for CD19) paired with m971 (moderate affinity for CD22, reducing steric competition). The CD22 arm provides safety net against CD19 loss while CD19 arm handles CD22-dim populations. Manufacturing uses standard lentiviral transduction; CAR expression typically 35-50%. Product infused as single defined-composition dose.
known_toxicities: CRS 82% (grade ≥3: 11%), ICANS 15%

name: Bispecific BCMA/GPRC5D Dual-Targeting CAR
text_summary: Dual-targeting CAR-T co-expressing two separate CAR constructs: anti-BCMA (from cilta-cel LCAR-B38M biparatopic binder) and anti-GPRC5D (nanobody-based), each with independent 4-1BB costimulation. This bicistronic design addresses the primary failure mode of BCMA CAR-T: BCMA antigen loss (23% of relapses). By targeting two independent myeloma antigens, complete tumor escape requires simultaneous loss of both — an event estimated at <2% probability. Preclinical data showed 100% tumor eradication in dual-antigen-positive xenograft models and 85% control of mixed (BCMA+/BCMA-) tumors. Manufacturing: dual CAR expression achieved via 2A peptide linkage in single lentiviral vector; co-expression rate 60-75%. Expected toxicities include both BCMA-related (CRS) and GPRC5D-related (nail/skin changes, dysgeusia). Phase I trials initiated 2024.
known_toxicities: Expected: CRS + nail/skin toxicity + dysgeusia

name: Armored IL-21-Secreting Anti-CD19 CAR-T
text_summary: Fourth-generation CAR-T (TRUCK) co-expressing membrane-bound IL-21 under constitutive promoter. IL-21 enhances: (1) CAR-T stemness — maintains Tscm phenotype during expansion (3-fold increase in TCF7+ cells); (2) anti-tumor immunity — recruits and activates endogenous NK cells and CD8+ T cells; (3) persistence — IL-21 signaling through STAT3 prevents exhaustion. Compared to standard CAR-T in preclinical models: 2.8x longer persistence, 2.1x higher tumor clearance in relapse rechallenge models. Unlike secreted IL-21 (systemic toxicity risk), membrane-bound form provides paracrine signaling only to cells in contact — limiting off-target immune activation. CAR design: FMC63 scFv + CD28 hinge + CD28 TM + 4-1BB + CD3-zeta + P2A + mbIL-21. Manufacturing: standard lentiviral; IL-21 expression confirmed by surface staining.
known_toxicities: Expected: CRS similar to standard; reduced relapse from enhanced persistence

name: PD-1 Knockout CRISPR Anti-CD19 CAR-T
text_summary: Anti-CD19 CAR-T cells with CRISPR/Cas9-mediated PDCD1 (PD-1) gene disruption. PD-1/PD-L1 signaling is a major brake on CAR-T function, particularly in tumors with high PD-L1 expression and in heavily pretreated patients with exhausted T cells. PDCD1 knockout efficiency: 85-95% by flow cytometry. In Phase I: ORR 83% with CR 67% (numerically superior to historical non-edited controls). CAR-T expansion 3.2x higher (Cmax) and persistence 2.8x longer (AUC0-90) than matched non-edited controls. Single-cell RNA-seq confirmed enrichment for Tscm and Tcm phenotypes. No autoimmune adverse events observed (a theoretical concern with checkpoint disruption). Manufacturing: electroporation of Cas9-sgRNA RNP complex targeting PDCD1 exon 1, followed by standard lentiviral transduction and expansion. Additional edits in development: LAG-3 knockout, TIM-3 knockout, dual PD-1/LAG-3 knockout.
known_toxicities: CRS 79% (grade ≥3: 17%), ICANS 13%. No autoimmune events

name: TRAC-Integrated Universal CAR (Allogeneic)
text_summary: Allogeneic anti-CD19 CAR-T with CAR transgene inserted at the TRAC locus via CRISPR/Cas9 + AAV6 donor template. Dual benefit: (1) TCR knockout prevents GvHD; (2) CAR expression under endogenous TCR promoter provides physiologic regulation (avoids tonic signaling from constitutive viral promoter). Additional edits: B2M knockout (avoids MHC-I-mediated rejection), CD52 knockout (enables anti-CD52 lymphodepletion without killing product). Knock-in efficiency: 35-45%. TCR knockout: >98%. Product: manufactured from healthy donor T cells with defined CD4:CD8 ratio. Off-the-shelf availability within 48 hours of prescription. In clinical testing (CTX110): ORR 58%, CR 37%. No GvHD. Host anti-product immune responses in 35% (limiting persistence). Next-gen: adding HLA-E knock-in + CD47 overexpression to evade NK-cell and macrophage-mediated rejection.
known_toxicities: CRS 56% (grade ≥3: 5%), ICANS 12%, GvHD 0%. Host rejection limiting persistence

name: SynNotch Logic-Gated AND-Gate CAR
text_summary: Synthetic Notch (SynNotch) receptor system enabling Boolean AND-gate logic: CAR-T activates only when both antigens are present on the same cell. Architecture: (1) SynNotch receptor recognizes Antigen A (e.g., GPC3) → triggers transcription of CAR targeting Antigen B (e.g., EpCAM). This prevents killing of cells expressing only one antigen — critical for solid tumors where each individual antigen may be expressed on some normal tissues but dual expression is tumor-specific. Preclinical: 100% tumor killing when both antigens present, <5% killing of single-antigen cells (vs 40-60% killing with standard OR-gate bispecific). Reduced on-target/off-tumor toxicity in normal tissue co-culture models. Limitations: delayed activation (4-8h for SynNotch-induced CAR expression), lower peak killing (60% of standard CAR at E:T 1:1), more complex manufacturing. Currently in preclinical development for HER2/mesothelin in mesothelioma and GPC3/EpCAM in liver cancer.
known_toxicities: Expected: reduced on-target/off-tumor toxicity; delayed activation kinetics

name: Adapter (FITC/Tag) Universal CAR Platform
text_summary: Universal adapter CAR system: CAR-T cells express an anti-FITC scFv CAR that recognizes FITC-conjugated targeting modules (tumor-specific antibodies labeled with FITC). This modular design enables: (1) rapid target switching without new CAR-T manufacturing; (2) dose-titratable activity (adjust adapter antibody dose); (3) safety switch (stop adapter infusion = CAR-T off); (4) multi-target sequential therapy (infuse different adapters sequentially). Platform tested with FITC-labeled anti-CD19, anti-CD20, anti-HER2, and anti-EGFR adapters. Preclinical: anti-tumor activity equivalent to standard CAR when adapter saturating. Clinical: Phase I with anti-CD19 FITC adapter showed ORR 52% — lower than standard CAR-T (likely from adapter pharmacokinetics and need for continuous infusion). Advantage: single manufacturing run enables treatment across multiple cancers by changing only the adapter molecule.
known_toxicities: CRS 35% (lower than standard, dose-titratable), adapter pharmacokinetics limit peak activity

name: CD7-Knockout Anti-CD7 Fratricide-Resistant CAR
text_summary: Anti-CD7 CAR-T for T-cell malignancies with CRISPR/Cas9-mediated CD7 gene disruption to prevent fratricide (CAR-T killing sibling CAR-T cells expressing CD7). Engineering: CD7 knockout via Cas9-RNP electroporation (efficiency 92-98%) followed by lentiviral transduction with anti-CD7 CAR. CAR design: humanized anti-CD7 scFv + CD28 hinge/TM + 4-1BB + CD3-zeta. CD7 knockout does not impair T-cell function: expansion, cytokine production, and cytotoxicity equivalent to unedited T cells. In Phase I for R/R T-ALL: CR/CRi 89%, MRD negativity 84%. Expected consequences: complete CD7+ T-cell depletion (requires antimicrobial prophylaxis), CD7+ NK cell depletion. Immune reconstitution requires bridge to allogeneic HSCT in most patients. Manufacturing: 7-10 day process from electroporation through harvest.
known_toxicities: CRS 75% (grade ≥3: 7%), complete T/NK depletion, bridge to transplant needed

name: Anti-BCMA with Gamma-Secretase Inhibitor Priming
text_summary: Standard anti-BCMA CAR-T (ide-cel backbone: anti-BCMA scFv from C11D5.3 + 4-1BB + CD3-zeta) combined with pre-treatment gamma-secretase inhibitor (GSI, nirogacestat 100mg BID for 7 days prior to CAR-T infusion). GSI blocks BCMA cleavage from myeloma cell surface, increasing surface BCMA density 2.5-fold (from median 3,000 to 7,500 MESF/cell) and reducing soluble BCMA (decoy) by 80%. Preclinical: GSI priming improved CAR-T cytotoxicity 3-fold against low-BCMA-density myeloma cells. Clinical Phase I/II: GSI priming + ide-cel: ORR 84% vs 73% (historical ide-cel alone), CR 52% vs 33%. Particularly beneficial for patients with low baseline BCMA (<2,000 MESF): ORR improved from 35% to 72%. GSI toxicity: GI (diarrhea 45%, grade ≥3: 8%), skin rash 22%. GSI discontinued at CAR-T infusion (transient use). This combination addresses a key resistance mechanism without modifying the CAR-T product itself.
known_toxicities: CRS similar to standard; GSI-related: diarrhea 45%, rash 22%

name: CAR-NK (CD19-Targeted Natural Killer Cell)
text_summary: Anti-CD19 CAR-NK cells derived from cord blood-expanded NK cells. Key advantages over CAR-T: (1) no GvHD risk (NK cells do not cause GvHD even when allogeneic); (2) off-the-shelf availability from banked cord blood units; (3) different cytokine profile — less IL-6 release, lower CRS risk; (4) intrinsic anti-tumor mechanisms (NKG2D, DNAM-1) provide additional killing beyond CAR. CAR construct: anti-CD19 scFv + 2B4 (NKR) + CD3-zeta + ectopic IL-15 (for in vivo persistence). The ectopic IL-15 is critical because NK cells require IL-15 for survival (unlike T cells which survive on IL-7/IL-15). Phase I/II (MD Anderson, NCT03056339): ORR 73% in CLL/lymphoma, CR 42%. CRS: 0% (vs 50-90% with CAR-T). No ICANS. Neurotoxicity: 0%. Limitation: shorter persistence than CAR-T (median 14 days detectable vs months for CAR-T); may require repeated dosing. Manufacturing: cord blood NK expansion (14-21 days), retroviral CAR transduction, IL-15 co-expression.
known_toxicities: CRS 0%, no ICANS, no GvHD. Limited persistence (14 days)

name: TCR-Engineered T Cells (NY-ESO-1 for Solid Tumors)
text_summary: Engineered T-cell receptor (TCR) therapy targeting NY-ESO-1 (cancer-testis antigen expressed in melanoma, synovial sarcoma, myxoid liposarcoma, NSCLC, ovarian). Unlike CARs that recognize surface proteins, engineered TCRs recognize intracellular peptides presented on MHC-I — dramatically expanding the targetable antigen space. TCR: affinity-enhanced NY-ESO-1-specific TCR (1G4 clone, HLA-A*02:01 restricted). Phase II results: synovial sarcoma ORR 50% (including durable CRs lasting >3 years), melanoma ORR 55%. HLA restriction limits eligible population to HLA-A*02:01 carriers (~40% of Caucasians, 10-15% of other ethnicities). Key risk: TCR cross-reactivity with MAGE-A3 in cardiac tissue caused fatal cardiac toxicity in early trials with different TCR — current TCR extensively safety-tested. Not a CAR but included because it represents the broader engineered cellular therapy space and shares manufacturing infrastructure.
known_toxicities: CRS rare (<10%); cardiac risk with certain TCR variants; HLA-restricted

name: Claudin18.2 Humanized scFv CAR (CT041)
text_summary: CT041 is a Claudin18.2-targeted CAR-T developed by CARsgen, the most clinically advanced solid tumor CAR-T product. CAR design: humanized anti-Claudin18.2 scFv (avoiding immunogenicity of murine scFv) + CD8alpha hinge + CD8alpha TM + 4-1BB + CD3-zeta. Claudin18.2 is a tight junction protein normally accessible only on the basolateral surface of gastric epithelial cells — in cancer, tight junction disruption exposes the epitope, providing tumor selectivity despite normal tissue expression. Key clinical data: gastric cancer ORR 57.4%, pancreatic cancer ORR 30.4%. Claudin18.2 maintained in 85% of post-treatment biopsies (low antigen escape). GI toxicity (on-target): 38% but manageable. Manufacturing: standard lentiviral, 10-14 day expansion. Patient selection: IHC staining for Claudin18.2 (≥70% of cells, ≥2+ intensity) required for enrollment. Approximately 50% of gastric cancers and 60% of pancreatic cancers are Claudin18.2 positive.
known_toxicities: CRS 83% (grade ≥3: 4.2%), GI toxicity 38%, minimal antigen escape

name: GPRC5D Nanobody-Based CAR
text_summary: Anti-GPRC5D CAR using single-domain antibody (VHH nanobody) instead of traditional scFv. Advantages of nanobody format: (1) smaller size (15 kDa vs 25 kDa scFv) — better epitope access to GPRC5D's small extracellular domain; (2) single-chain — no VH-VL mispairing or aggregation; (3) lower immunogenicity (camelid framework humanized to >90%); (4) superior stability and folding. GPRC5D targeting provides BCMA-independent myeloma kill — critical for patients who relapse after BCMA CAR-T (23% show BCMA loss). In Phase I: ORR 71% overall, 60% in BCMA-refractory. GPRC5D-specific toxicities: nail changes 47%, dysgeusia 44%, skin dryness 34% — all reflecting hair follicle expression and all reversible. The nanobody format showed superior expansion compared to scFv-based anti-GPRC5D constructs in preclinical head-to-head comparison (2.1x higher Cmax).
known_toxicities: CRS 81% (grade ≥3: 6%), nail 47%, dysgeusia 44%, skin 34% — all reversible

name: iCasp9 Safety Switch Anti-CD19 CAR-T
text_summary: Anti-CD19 CAR-T incorporating inducible caspase-9 (iCasp9) suicide switch for safety. iCasp9 is a modified caspase-9 fused to FK506-binding protein (FKBP12-F36V) that dimerizes upon administration of AP1903 (rimiducid), triggering rapid apoptosis of CAR-T cells. Within 30 minutes of AP1903 infusion: >95% of iCasp9+ cells eliminated. Clinical utility: emergency treatment of refractory grade 4 CRS/ICANS not responding to tocilizumab + steroids; management of severe on-target/off-tumor toxicity; controlled termination after achieving remission. Tested in allogeneic HSCT setting: iCasp9 activation eliminated alloreactive T cells and resolved GvHD within 24h in 90% of patients. For CAR-T: safety switch provides insurance against unpredictable severe toxicity, enabling more aggressive dosing strategies. Trade-off: iCasp9 construct adds 1.2 kb to transgene, slightly reducing transduction efficiency. AP1903 is available as IND for emergency use.
known_toxicities: iCasp9 activation eliminates >95% CAR-T within 30 min. Standard CRS/ICANS rates pre-activation

name: Multi-Antigen Sequential Targeting Platform (CD19 → CD22 → BCMA)
text_summary: Adaptive multi-antigen targeting system using serial CAR-T infusions against different antigens based on disease evolution. Protocol: initial infusion of anti-CD19 CAR-T → at first sign of CD19-negative relapse, administer anti-CD22 CAR-T → if CD22 escape, administer anti-BCMA CAR-T (for myeloma) or bispecific. This sequential approach is distinct from bispecific CARs: it allows dose optimization for each target, real-time adaptation to antigen landscape changes, and use of best-in-class single-target CARs rather than compromised bispecific designs. In a retrospective analysis of 45 patients with serial CAR-T: 78% achieved CR with first product, 55% of relapsers achieved second CR with different-target CAR-T, overall 12-month OS was 72%. Challenges: cumulative CRS burden, T-cell fitness degradation with successive products, cost. Emerging alternative: pre-manufactured allogeneic CARs against multiple targets, enabling same-day switching.
known_toxicities: Cumulative CRS with each infusion; T-cell quality degrades with each product

name: EGFRvIII Peptidomimetic CAR with Enhanced Specificity
text_summary: Third-generation anti-EGFRvIII CAR using peptidomimetic binder (DARPin, designed ankyrin repeat protein) instead of scFv. EGFRvIII is a tumor-specific EGFR deletion mutant (deletion of exons 2-7) found in 25-30% of GBM. DARPin advantages: (1) extremely high specificity for EGFRvIII vs wild-type EGFR (>1000-fold selectivity); (2) no binding to normal EGFR (eliminates skin/GI toxicity risk); (3) high stability and expression. CAR architecture: anti-EGFRvIII DARPin + CD28 hinge/TM + CD28 + 4-1BB + CD3-zeta (third generation with dual costimulation). Third-gen design chosen because GBM requires maximal T-cell activation in highly suppressive TME. Preclinical: complete tumor eradication in orthotopic GBM models. Key challenge: EGFRvIII expression heterogeneity (only 25-30% of GBM cells express it) — resistant clones lack EGFRvIII. Combination with anti-IL13Ra2 or anti-B7-H3 being explored for multi-antigen coverage.
known_toxicities: CRS expected mild (CNS delivery); EGFRvIII heterogeneity limits durability
